jokes.text: I used to be intimidated by the British. I thought they were superior in taste, style, and breeding. Then I saw Benny Hill.
jokes.setup: Will appears to be making a sophisticated observation about British cultural superiority
jokes.punchline: The lowbrow reference to Benny Hill's slapstick comedy deflates the pretentious setup
jokes.explanation: Strong contrast comedy - sets up sophisticated cultural commentary then punctures it with lowbrow reference. Perfect character voice for Will.

jokes.text: Master Carlton. First of all, I like Benny Hill.
jokes.setup: Will's dismissive comment about Benny Hill
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's dignified but defensive response reveals his guilty pleasure
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Geoffrey's human side beneath his formal exterior.

jokes.text: Geoffrey, mon! Do the limbo with me.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey just finished explaining the need for quiet dignity
jokes.punchline: Will immediately does the opposite by suggesting undignified limbo dancing
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing - Will immediately contradicts Geoffrey's dignity request with something undignified.

jokes.text: This is precisely what cannot happen when Lord Fowler arrives.
jokes.setup: Will's limbo suggestion
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's exasperated but understated response
jokes.explanation: Solid straight-man reaction, but fairly predictable.

jokes.text: I'll be at the peak of Schweppervescence.
jokes.setup: Will claiming he knows how to deal with royalty
jokes.punchline: Made-up word combining 'effervescence' with Schweppes (British tonic brand)
jokes.explanation: Clever invented word that shows Will's creativity with language while staying in character.

jokes.text: Just around the corner. - Where around the corner? Nevada.
jokes.setup: Will claims the concert is 'just around the corner'
jokes.punchline: Nevada is obviously not 'around the corner' from Bel-Air
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection with perfect economy - the geographic impossibility is the entire joke.

jokes.text: Uncle Phil, mon! You are generous, fair-minded, and very sexy.
jokes.setup: Will trying to butter up Uncle Phil for money
jokes.punchline: The 'very sexy' escalates the flattery to uncomfortable territory
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke - the sexual compliment to his uncle crosses a line for comedic effect.

jokes.text: A mysterious dent, a broken taillight... a mural of Nia Peeples painted on the side.
jokes.setup: Phil listing previous car damage from Will's concert trips
jokes.punchline: Escalates from normal damage to bizarrely specific mural reference
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with highly specific, absurd detail. Nia Peeples reference is perfectly random and memorable.

jokes.text: I swear to you guys that was on the car when I got in it.
jokes.setup: Phil's accusation about the Nia Peeples mural
jokes.punchline: Will's implausible denial of responsibility
jokes.explanation: Solid follow-up to the mural joke, showing Will's tendency to deny obvious guilt.

jokes.text: It was a bit choppy and quite long. And of course, the food was horrid and the movie was a dreadful bore. I wouldn't want to make that journey too often.
jokes.setup: Phil asking how the flight was
jokes.punchline: Lord Fowler's relentlessly negative but understated British complaints
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment - shows Lord Fowler's tendency toward polite but persistent complaining.

jokes.text: Philip, why are you talking like that? That ridiculous British accent. Whenever you're around an English person, you slip right into it.
jokes.setup: Phil has been unconsciously adopting a British accent
jokes.punchline: Vivian calls out this embarrassing behavior pattern
jokes.explanation: Relatable behavior called out - many people unconsciously mirror accents, making this universally funny.

jokes.text: The first three months Geoffrey was here, I could barely understand you.
jokes.setup: Vivian calling out Phil's accent mimicking
jokes.punchline: Reveals this has been a long-term embarrassing pattern
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that deepens the joke by showing it's a chronic problem, not just tonight.

jokes.text: I have no idea what you're talking about. Sir, madam, care to join us for tea? Right-o.
jokes.setup: Phil denying he does the British accent
jokes.punchline: Immediately slips back into British accent and mannerisms
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic contradiction - Phil proves Vivian's point immediately after denying it.

jokes.text: Spending all your time shopping, going to lunch with your friends... and just sitting around being waited on hand and foot. I wonder what that would be like.
jokes.setup: Hilary describing what she thinks Lady life is like
jokes.punchline: She's describing her own privileged lifestyle without realizing it
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - Hilary's complete lack of self-awareness is the joke. Expertly crafted irony.

jokes.text: One can only imagine.
jokes.setup: Hilary's oblivious description of privileged life
jokes.punchline: Lady Penelope's dry response suggests she knows exactly how clueless Hilary is
jokes.explanation: Subtle but effective - Lady Penelope's restraint makes Hilary's obliviousness even funnier.

jokes.text: Actually, I go to a small convent school and the nuns work us very hard. We scrub the floors, cook the meals and on the weekends, we do all the laundry.
jokes.setup: Expectation that Lady Penelope lives a pampered life
jokes.punchline: Reality is she lives like Cinderella at convent school
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that subverts class expectations and sets up Lady Penelope's later rebellion.

jokes.text: What a gyp.
jokes.setup: Lady Penelope's description of her hard life at convent school
jokes.punchline: Ashley's blunt, American assessment of the situation
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - Ashley cuts through the politeness with honest reaction.

jokes.text: We've made some fascinating strides in maritime legislation. I'll go into greater details at dinner.
jokes.setup: Lord Fowler trying to make dinner plans sound appealing
jokes.punchline: Promises boring maritime law discussion as dinner entertainment
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment - shows Lord Fowler's obliviousness to what others find interesting.

jokes.text: Is tonight a school night? No. I already thought of that.
jokes.setup: The family dreading dinner with Lord Fowler
jokes.punchline: Ashley desperately looking for any excuse to escape, Carlton blocking it
jokes.explanation: Relatable family dynamics - kid looking for escape, sibling blocking it.

jokes.text: Opera? I thought she said 'Oprah.'
jokes.setup: Will agreeing to take Lady Penelope to the opera
jokes.punchline: Reveals he misheard 'opera' as 'Oprah' (talk show)
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay that's perfectly in character for Will - street smart but culturally unfamiliar with high society.

jokes.text: Spider-Man comics. Watchman. Goobers, Raisinets.
jokes.setup: Will packing for what he thinks is going to see Oprah
jokes.punchline: Packing movie theater snacks and comics for the opera
jokes.explanation: Good callback that extends the Oprah confusion joke by showing Will's inappropriate preparation.

jokes.text: Did you see what I did with my spitball straw?
jokes.setup: Will packing for what he thinks is a talk show
jokes.punchline: Looking for a spitball straw as opera equipment
jokes.explanation: Continues the callback with another inappropriate item for the opera setting.

jokes.text: Mr. Belvesneer.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey trying to give Will serious advice
jokes.punchline: Will mispronounces Geoffrey's name as 'Mr. Belvesneer' (like Belvedere)
jokes.explanation: Typical Will mispronunciation showing his casual disrespect for formality.

jokes.text: How hard can it be? I'll take her to the opera, cop a few 'Z's'... bring her back home.
jokes.setup: Will claiming the task is easy
jokes.punchline: Plans to sleep through the opera he's supposed to be escorting her to
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Will's casual attitude and poor understanding of responsibility.

jokes.text: if 895 members of the royal family instantly died... she would be the next queen of England.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey explaining Lady Penelope's importance
jokes.punchline: The morbidly specific scenario of 895 royal deaths needed for her to become queen
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd and darkly funny - the specificity of '895 deaths' makes it memorable and quotable.

jokes.text: Now, where does that put me?
jokes.setup: Geoffrey's explanation of Lady Penelope's royal significance
jokes.punchline: Will's self-centered concern about his own position
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Will's typical self-centered worldview even in serious moments.

jokes.text: Does this restaurant have a bar? If it doesn't, we'll build one.
jokes.setup: Lord Fowler's incredibly boring dinner conversation
jokes.punchline: The family's desperate need for alcohol to cope
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - Phil's willingness to build a bar shows extreme desperation.

jokes.text: I don't feel so good. Don't even try it, Ashley. We're all in this together.
jokes.setup: The family trapped with Lord Fowler's boring conversation
jokes.punchline: Ashley trying to fake illness, Carlton blocking her escape
jokes.explanation: Relatable family dynamics - no one escapes shared suffering.

jokes.text: I thought the old buzzard would never leave.
jokes.setup: Lady Penelope has been playing the perfect innocent daughter
jokes.punchline: Immediately drops the act and calls her father an 'old buzzard'
jokes.explanation: Excellent character reversal - complete transformation from innocent to rebellious in one line.

jokes.text: Sod off, mother superior.
jokes.setup: Will lecturing her about smoking being bad
jokes.punchline: British profanity calling Will 'mother superior' (nun reference)
jokes.explanation: Clever insult that references her convent school background while showing her rebellious side.

jokes.text: Don't nobody call me a mother nothing.
jokes.setup: Lady Penelope calling Will 'mother superior'
jokes.punchline: Will's street vernacular response to the nun comparison
jokes.explanation: Good character voice showing Will's street attitude clashing with the nun reference.

jokes.text: Who are you, Dr. Jekyll and Patty Duke?
jokes.setup: Lady Penelope's dramatic personality change
jokes.punchline: Mixing Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde with Patty Duke (who played twins)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant pop culture mashup joke - combines classic literature with 90s TV reference perfectly.

jokes.text: Don't be such a wanker.
jokes.setup: Lady Penelope continuing her rebellious act
jokes.punchline: Using British slang profanity casually
jokes.explanation: Continues character development but less impact than previous lines.

jokes.text: The Meat Hook? That's the roughest bar in L.A. Super. That's what Lady Cicely told me.
jokes.setup: Lady Penelope wants to go clubbing
jokes.punchline: She specifically chose the roughest bar based on another aristocrat's recommendation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing wealthy British girls networking about rough American bars.

jokes.text: See, I'm a plainclothes nun. Yeah, that's right. They call me Sister Sledge.
jokes.setup: Will trying to assert authority over Lady Penelope
jokes.punchline: Invents being a 'plainclothes nun' called 'Sister Sledge' (music group reference)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd logic combining police work, religious authority, and 80s music group. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Man, this babysitting thing is easy.
jokes.setup: Will thinks he's successfully handled Lady Penelope
jokes.punchline: Famous last words - she's already escaped
jokes.explanation: Classic dramatic irony - audience knows she's escaped while Will declares victory.

jokes.text: Lady, come back with my car!
jokes.setup: Will's overconfident declaration about babysitting being easy
jokes.punchline: Immediate reversal as she steals the car
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing - immediate reversal of Will's confidence with escalating consequences.

jokes.text: Yo, man. Should I get the bouncer? I am the bouncer.
jokes.setup: Will asking if they should get the bouncer
jokes.punchline: The guy he's talking to IS the bouncer
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline showing how rough the bar is

jokes.text: I think I saw that guy on Unsolved Mysteries.
jokes.setup: Will observing the rough crowd at the Meat Hook bar
jokes.punchline: Suggesting someone looks like an unsolved crime perpetrator
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference that establishes the bar's dangerous atmosphere through humor.

jokes.text: Loosen up, homes. Come on. Get up and dance. Shake your moneymaker.
jokes.setup: British aristocrat Lady Penelope in a rough American bar
jokes.punchline: Using American slang like 'homes' and 'shake your moneymaker'
jokes.explanation: Funny cultural clash - British aristocrat trying to sound street tough with American slang.

jokes.text: You can't touch this!
jokes.setup: Lady Penelope dancing wildly on the bar
jokes.punchline: Quoting MC Hammer while being a British aristocrat
jokes.explanation: Pop culture reference fits the character's rebellion but is somewhat predictable.

jokes.text: You're dancing with my woman. No. You got it all wrong. I'm not dancing with her. I'm trying to get her to go home with me. I'll bet you are.
jokes.setup: Big tough guy thinks Will is hitting on his girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Will's honest explanation makes it sound worse
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection where Will's attempt to defuse the situation makes it worse through poor word choice.

jokes.text: which, in maritime law circles... has come to be known as 'The Year of the Trout.'
jokes.setup: Lord Fowler continuing his maritime law lecture
jokes.punchline: 1907 being called 'The Year of the Trout' in maritime law circles
jokes.explanation: Absurdly specific maritime law reference that makes the boring subject even more ridiculous.

jokes.text: I saw that sweater first!
jokes.setup: Restaurant scene with Lord Fowler's boring lecture
jokes.punchline: Background fight over a sweater interrupting the scene
jokes.explanation: Random background gag that provides brief relief from boring dinner but lacks craft.

jokes.text: Wasn't there quite a row in the House of Lords in 1912... when they repealed the Tuna Laws?
jokes.setup: Geoffrey trying to engage with Lord Fowler's maritime law discussion
jokes.punchline: Making up equally ridiculous 'Tuna Laws' to keep the conversation going
jokes.explanation: Geoffrey playing along with the maritime law theme while trying to be polite, creating absurd 'Tuna Laws.'

jokes.text: A lobotomy.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey asking if anyone wants anything during the boring dinner
jokes.punchline: Vivian sarcastically requesting a lobotomy to escape the tedium
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and dark humor - one word that perfectly captures everyone's desperation.

jokes.text: G, the Lady is a tramp.
jokes.setup: Will trying to explain Lady Penelope's behavior to Geoffrey
jokes.punchline: Reference to the song 'The Lady Is a Tramp' while literally describing her behavior
jokes.explanation: Clever dual meaning using classic song title to describe actual trampy behavior.

jokes.text: I swear on a stack of TV Guides.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey asking if Will is telling the truth
jokes.punchline: Will swearing on TV Guides instead of Bibles
jokes.explanation: Perfect character voice - Will's pop culture-obsessed worldview where TV Guides are sacred.

jokes.text: I'm not going to get to see Ziggy.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey worrying about his career being ruined
jokes.punchline: Will's self-centered focus on missing the concert
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency showing Will's priorities even in crisis situations.

jokes.text: To the Batmobile.
jokes.setup: Will and Geoffrey needing to rush to rescue Lady Penelope
jokes.punchline: Will treating their car like the Batmobile for their 'heroic' mission
jokes.explanation: Classic pop culture reference that fits Will's tendency to dramatize situations.

jokes.text: My cousin Roddy has just been suddenly struck with malaria. I must go to quarantine immediately.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey needing to excuse himself from dinner to help Will
jokes.punchline: Bizarrely specific and dramatic fake excuse about malaria quarantine
jokes.explanation: Geoffrey's overly dramatic and specific lie shows his panic while maintaining his formal speech patterns.

jokes.text: He could be hiding her in his fist.
jokes.setup: Looking for Lady Penelope and seeing the enormous tough guy Otis
jokes.punchline: Suggesting he's so big he could hide a person in his fist
jokes.explanation: Funny exaggeration of the guy's size with vivid imagery.

jokes.text: What have you done with Lady Penelope, you knuckle-dragging swine?
jokes.setup: Geoffrey confronting the dangerous tough guy
jokes.punchline: Using formal British insults ('knuckle-dragging swine') on a street thug
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - Geoffrey maintaining his formal speaking style even when confronting criminals.

jokes.text: Don't press him, G. You saw what he did to Tokyo.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey challenging the enormous tough guy to a fight
jokes.punchline: Comparing him to Godzilla destroying Tokyo
jokes.explanation: Great callback extending the Godzilla comparison with specific reference to Tokyo destruction.

jokes.text: In my youth, I was quite a Greco-Roman wrestler.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey facing an obviously dangerous opponent
jokes.punchline: Revealing his background in formal wrestling (implying he's outmatched)
jokes.explanation: Good character detail that sets up his upcoming defeat while showing his proper background.

jokes.text: You're ugly. And you, sir, are fat. Or was it the other way round?
jokes.setup: Lord Fowler telling what he thinks is a witty historical anecdote
jokes.punchline: Can't remember which historical figure said what insult
jokes.explanation: Shows Lord Fowler's tendency to tell pointless stories with forgotten punchlines.

jokes.text: Mommy. God bless you, sweetheart. What's the matter, baby? I can't sleep.
jokes.setup: The family suffering through Lord Fowler's endless stories
jokes.punchline: Ashley pretending to be a sleepy child to escape
jokes.explanation: Callback to Ashley's earlier escape attempts, now trying a different strategy.

jokes.text: You're not going anywhere, woman. Sorry, Mommy. I tried. Try again in 10 minutes.
jokes.setup: Ashley trying to escape using the child act
jokes.punchline: Phil blocking her escape, Ashley breaking character, then going back into it
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the escape theme with Ashley's commitment to her act.

jokes.text: God. If you don't know, I have no idea.
jokes.setup: Lord Fowler losing track of his own boring story
jokes.punchline: Phil's exasperated response to the pointless narrative
jokes.explanation: Good frustrated reaction to the endless, pointless storytelling.

jokes.text: You're young. You have your whole life ahead of you. It's best I bear the brunt of the punishment.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey and Will discussing who should tell Lord Fowler about losing his daughter
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey nobly sacrificing himself while Will immediately agrees
jokes.explanation: Shows Geoffrey's noble character and sets up Will's selfish response.

jokes.text: You got a point there. Good luck.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey's noble sacrifice speech
jokes.punchline: Will immediately abandoning him to his fate
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Will's self-preservation instincts overriding loyalty.

jokes.text: Damn malaria.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey's earlier fake excuse about cousin Roddy's malaria
jokes.punchline: Lord Fowler's casual acknowledgment of the fake disease
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Geoffrey's earlier absurd excuse.

jokes.text: Geoffrey, it's not more gambling debts, is it? I thought we had that productive chat.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey trying to confess about losing Lady Penelope
jokes.punchline: Lord Fowler assumes it's about Geoffrey's apparently recurring gambling problem
jokes.explanation: Reveals unexpected character detail about Geoffrey having gambling issues.

jokes.text: But around the third act, Will was starting to fade... so I told the poor dear to go on home.
jokes.setup: Explaining Will's absence from opera
jokes.punchline: Her fake concern covering for the real chaos
jokes.explanation: Excellent lie that perfectly covers the truth while maintaining her character

jokes.text: That's the mark of a great storyteller. Always leave them wanting more.
jokes.setup: Lord Fowler refusing to tell more boring stories
jokes.punchline: Phil sarcastically calling him a great storyteller for stopping
jokes.explanation: Perfect sarcasm - Phil grateful that Lord Fowler is finally stopping his terrible stories.

jokes.text: Next time I'm in town, we'll have to go to the ballet.
jokes.setup: Lord Fowler suggesting another cultural outing
jokes.punchline: Threatening them with ballet next time
jokes.explanation: Shows Lord Fowler's continued obliviousness to how boring he is.

jokes.text: Since that explanation that Lady Penelope rattled off... was obviously a complete crock, what really did happen tonight?
jokes.setup: Phil accepting Will's success publicly
jokes.punchline: Immediately acknowledging he knows it's all lies
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Phil's awareness and parental wisdom

jokes.text: Since that explanation that Lady Penelope rattled off... was obviously a complete crock, what really did happen tonight?
jokes.setup: Lady Penelope gave a perfect cover story about the opera
jokes.punchline: Phil immediately sees through it and wants the real story
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the episode's resolution showing Phil isn't fooled by the obvious lie.

jokes.text: You should sit down. It's going to be a long story, Uncle Phil.
jokes.setup: Phil asking what really happened
jokes.punchline: Will warning him to sit down for the long explanation
jokes.explanation: Sets up the episode ending by implying the wild story that would follow.

jokes.text: No. Never mind. Enjoy the concert.
jokes.setup: Will warning the story will be long
jokes.punchline: Phil immediately changing his mind and not wanting to hear it
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending showing Phil's exhaustion from the evening and preference for ignorance.
